perturbation.original_text: 9. Compensation (a) In consideration of the rights granted by Individual hereunder, Lifeway agrees to pay Individual a royalty (the "Royalty") equal to $0.02 for each Lifeway product or individual item sold by Lifeway during each calendar month of the Term bearing Individual's first name, last name or other identifying personal characteristics; provided, however, the Royalty will cease being paid upon the death of Individual. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary herein, the Royalty will not exceed $50,000 in any month. All undisputed Royalty payments shall be made in U.S. dollars in cash or to the order of Individual and shall be due and payable within thirty (30) days after the end of each calendar month for sales during the previous month. (b) All amounts due under this Agreement are net of any applicable taxes, duties, fees or governmental charges, including without limitation, transfer tax, sales tax, use tax, consumption tax, value-added tax, excise tax, import tax, export tax, and custom duties (collectively "Taxes"). Accordingly, Lifeway will be solely responsible for the payment of all Taxes, together with any related liabilities (including interest and penalties), imposed upon it by law or any governmental authority for which Lifeway is primarily liable. (c) The Royalty due hereunder shall be paid to Individual at the address set forth above.
perturbation.changed_text:
14. [Intentionally Omitted.]

9. Compensation (a) In consideration of the rights granted by Individual hereunder, Lifeway agrees to pay Individual a royalty (the "Royalty") equal to $0.02 for each Lifeway product or individual item sold by Lifeway during each calendar month of the Term bearing Individual's first name, last name or other identifying personal characteristics; provided, however, the Royalty will cease being paid upon the death of Individual. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary herein, the Royalty will not exceed $1,000 in any month. All undisputed Royalty payments shall be made in U.S. dollars in cryptocurrency to the order of Individual and shall be due and payable within one hundred and twenty (120) days after the end of each calendar quarter for sales during the previous quarter. (b) All amounts due under this Agreement are inclusive of any applicable taxes, duties, fees or governmental charges, including without limitation, transfer tax, sales tax, use tax, consumption tax, value-added tax, excise tax, import tax, export tax, and custom duties (collectively "Taxes"). Accordingly, the Individual will be solely responsible for the payment of all Taxes, together with any related liabilities (including interest and penalties), imposed upon it by law or any governmental authority for which the Individual is primarily liable. (c) The Royalty due hereunder shall be paid to Individual at an address to be determined at a later date.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the compensation details and changing the terms such as payment method, the time frame and liability will cause it to be overlooked and make it difficult for the individual to claim their compensation. Additionally, there is now a discrepancy on who is liable to pay taxes.
perturbation.law_explanation: 26 U.S. Code § 6041 requires businesses to report payments of $600 or more to individuals. The change to paying in cryptocurrency and stating that the individual is solely responsible for taxes, along with the delayed and vaguely defined address, obfuscates Lifeway's responsibility to report payments and potentially shifts the tax burden improperly, hindering compliance with tax reporting requirements and potentially evading tax liability.

Information at source (a) Payments of $600 or more All persons engaged in a trade or business and making payment in the course of such trade or business to another person, of rent, salaries, wages, premiums, annuities, compensations, remunerations, emoluments,
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts 26 U.S. Code § 6041. The original text specifies Lifeway is responsible for taxes and payments will be made in cash, making it straightforward to track payments and report them as required by law if they exceed $600. The modified text shifts the tax burden to the Individual, stipulates payment in cryptocurrency and delays the payment schedule. These changes obscure Lifeway's responsibility to report payments, potentially leading to non-compliance with tax reporting requirements and possibly evading tax liability. The address for royalty payment is also vague and undefined, hindering compliance with tax reporting requirements. This falls under the definition of structural flaws: Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 15. Choice of Law and Forum - This Agreement will be construed and governed in accordance with the laws of the State of Illinois, without regard to conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text:
4. Right to Edit - Lifeway may modify and vary the Marketing Materials produced hereunder, as it may elect, and to make additional versions of such Marketing Materials in any manner without approval from Individual; provided that Individual shall not be required to render any services in connection with the production of such modifications or variations. Moreover, Individual understands and agrees that Lifeway may use the Marketing Materials with or without her name.

15. Choice of Law and Forum - This Agreement will be construed and governed in accordance with the laws of the Principality of Sealand, without regard to conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the choice of law clause to the 'Right to Edit' section hides the legal jurisdiction governing the agreement. Changing the jurisdiction to the Principality of Sealand, a micronation with questionable legal standing, renders the contract ambiguous and difficult to enforce in a recognized legal system.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 outlines the requirements for diversity jurisdiction in federal courts, including the need for complete diversity of citizenship between plaintiffs and defendants and a minimum amount in controversy. Selecting the Principality of Sealand as the governing law raises questions about the enforceability of the contract in U.S. courts, since Sealand is not a recognized sovereign state, making it difficult to establish proper jurisdiction or determine applicable legal principles, and thus undermining the potential for diversity jurisdiction.

Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between- (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects o
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between— (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state, except that the district courts shall not have original jurisdiction under this subsection of an action between citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state who are lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States and are domiciled in the same State; (3) citizens of different States and in which citizens or subjects of a foreign state are additional parties; and (4) a foreign state, defined in section 1603(a) of this title , as plaintiff and citizens of a State or of different States. (b) Except when express provision therefor is otherwise made in a statute of the United States, where the plaintiff who files the case originally in the Federal courts is finally adjudged to be entitled to recover less than the sum or value of $75,000, computed without regard to any setoff or counterclaim to which the defendant may be adjudged to be entitled, and exclusive of interest and costs, the district court may deny costs to the plaintiff and, in addition, may impose costs on the plaintiff. (c) For the purposes of this section and section 1441 of this title — (1) a corporation shall be deemed to be a citizen of every State and foreign state by which it has been incorporated and of the State or foreign state where it has its principal place of business, except that in any direct action against the insurer of a policy or contract of liability insura
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts legal standards by changing the governing law to the Principality of Sealand. Sealand is not a recognized sovereign state, which creates uncertainty about the contract's enforceability in established legal systems. The Law Explanation highlights this issue by pointing out that it undermines the potential for diversity jurisdiction under 28 U.S. Code § 1332, because Sealand's status makes it difficult to establish proper jurisdiction. The provided law snippets from 28 USC 1332 confirm the requirements for diversity jurisdiction. The change introduces ambiguity regarding which legal system would adjudicate disputes, effectively weakening the legal framework governing the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Release - Individual hereby releases and discharges Lifeway from any and all claims, demands, or causes of action in law or equity that he or she may have or may hereafter acquire, including without limitation in connection with any prior use, reuse, publication, reproduction, performance, copy, creation of derivative works, exhibition, broadcast, and display of the name, image and likeness of Individual and any and all claims for libel, slander, invasion of privacy, copyright or trademark violation, right of publicity, or false light, that may arise out of or in connection with the use of the Marketing Materials in accordance with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text:
14. [Intentionally Omitted.]

3. Release - Individual hereby releases and discharges Lifeway from any and all claims, demands, or causes of action in law or equity that he or she may have or may hereafter acquire, including without limitation in connection with any prior use, reuse, publication, reproduction, performance, copy, creation of derivative works, exhibition, broadcast, and display of the name, image and likeness of Individual and any and all claims for libel, slander, invasion of privacy, copyright or trademark violation, right of publicity, or false light, that may arise out of or in connection with the use of the Marketing Materials in accordance with this Agreement. This release specifically includes any claims arising from Lifeway's gross negligence or intentional misconduct.
perturbation.explanation: Adding language that releases claims arising from 'Lifeway's gross negligence or intentional misconduct' could be considered unconscionable or against public policy, particularly in jurisdictions that do not allow parties to contract away liability for such egregious conduct.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Torts § 496B addresses express assumption of risk, which is related to exculpatory clauses. While it doesn't directly prohibit all such clauses, courts often scrutinize them, especially when they attempt to release liability for gross negligence or intentional torts. Adding the clause about releasing Lifeway from gross negligence and intentional misconduct could render the release unenforceable under the principles outlined in the Restatement and as applied in various state laws, because many jurisdictions consider it against public policy to allow parties to contract away liability for such conduct. (Note that the second URL requires a LexisNexis subscription, but provides supporting legal context).

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a release of liability for 'Lifeway's gross negligence or intentional misconduct.' The Law Explanation suggests this could be unenforceable as against public policy in many jurisdictions. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is empty, and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a sign-in page for LexisNexis (not providing direct legal text), the Law Explanation points to the Restatement (Second) of Torts § 496B and principles against contracting away liability for egregious conduct. The Perturbation Explanation also supports this, noting the potential unconscionability of releasing claims arising from gross negligence or intentional misconduct. Therefore, by attempting to release Lifeway from such liability, the modified text contradicts the general legal principle that parties cannot contract away responsibility for gross negligence or intentional torts, making the contradiction impactful.
